student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Reading the Fine Print -- When the Standard Moved and Nobody Told You
lesson.menu_desc: Quarenta unidades certificadas para a revisão C de uma norma que um amendment já havia substituído: como reconstruir a sequência de um non-conformance no past perfect e como fixar a letra miúda (provision, wording, order of precedence, loophole) antes de assinar -- 36 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 13 -- Reading the Fine Print
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 13 -- Reading the Fine Print
lesson.grammar_point: past perfect and past perfect continuous
phases: The Language of the Fine Print

listenings.text: João, it is Sabine. I have read your non-conformance report, and before the meeting I want to give you our side of the sequence, honestly, because I think the order of events matters more than either of us wants to admit. When your purchase order reached us in January, revision C of the standard was current, and that is the revision we built the axle counters to. What none of us knew was that the committee had already approved an amendment in December, and that amendment had superseded revision C and rewritten one provision in Annex D. It was not published on the portal until March. So by the time it appeared, we had been manufacturing to the old revision for almost three months, and we had already shipped the first batch. Now, your contract stipulated the standard in its current revision, and yes, strictly, the current revision had changed before we shipped. But nobody on either side had checked the portal, and nobody had flagged it. I am not going to pretend we had caught it and stayed silent, because we had not. What I am asking is that we agree on the timeline first, because when you read our report and yours together, you will see the discrepancy was shared. The wording left a grey area, both of us fell into it, and I would rather fix the annex reference in the contract than spend six months arguing about whose loophole it was.

Three rules, before you sign anything that refers to a technical standard, because the money is never in the clause you read, it is in the one you assumed. First rule. A standard is not a fixed thing. It has revisions, it has amendments, and an amendment can supersede the revision you certified to without changing the number on the cover. When a contract says the current revision, current means current on the day of delivery, not the day you signed. Check which revision is actually in force before you cut metal, because by the time the discrepancy shows up at the factory acceptance test, you will have built to a document that no longer exists. Second rule. Read the annexes. Everybody reads the main clause and nobody reads Annex D, and Annex D is exactly where the committee hides the change. The devil is in the details, and the details live in the fine print at the back. Third rule, and this one settles disputes. When something goes wrong, reconstruct the sequence in the past perfect, event by event, on paper. Who had done what, by when. What had already happened before the inspector opened the file. What each side had been doing while the other assumed something different. Nine times out of ten, the moment you lay the timeline out honestly, the liability stops being an argument and becomes a date. And a date is a thing two engineers can actually agree on.

patterns.title: Four ways to put one past event before another
cards: The past before the past. When you say what had happened before another past moment, the earlier action goes into the past perfect. It is the tense that fixes the ORDER, and in a non-conformance the order is the whole argument. "By the time we flagged it, the supplier had already shipped the units."
HOW LONG, up to that past point

It measures the duration that had built up before the past moment, and it is how a report shows the problem was not new. "They had been manufacturing to the old revision for three months before the amendment reached them."
by the time / before / when + past
These words set the LATER past moment; the earlier action then takes the past perfect. "By the time the audit began, revision C had already been superseded."
The words that live inside it
already = it had happened before then; never / not yet = it had not happened up to then. "We had never received the amendment, and they had already closed the file."

patterns.text: "When we caught it, the supplier shipped the units" says the two things happened together. "When we caught it, the supplier HAD already shipped the units" says the shipping came first. Same facts, one extra word: and in a dispute, the order is the liability.

items.situation: The supplier says the units were compliant when they left the factory. Put the two events in order and show which one came first.
tips: When the units left the factory, revision C had already been superseded by the amendment.
So they had been manufacturing to a withdrawn revision for three months before anyone noticed.

items.situation: Explain, in one sentence, why your inspector only caught the discrepancy at the factory acceptance test.
tips: By the time our inspector opened Annex D, the units had already been assembled and packed.
Nobody had cross-checked which revision was current, so the discrepancy had gone unnoticed for weeks.

items.situation: The supplier claims the contract never named a specific revision. State what the contract had actually stipulated.
tips: The contract had stipulated the standard in its current revision, and by the shipping date the current revision had changed.
The wording had not fixed a number, so the current revision governed, and that one had already moved.

items.situation: State clearly what should have been checked before production, and had not.
tips: Before production started, someone should have confirmed the normative reference, and nobody had.
By the time the amendment was published, we had not updated the specification, and neither had they.

items.situation: There is a grey area: the amendment changed a provision in an annex, not in the main clause. Describe how that came about.
tips: The amendment had revised the annex, but the main clause had stayed the same, so each side had read only the part it knew.
The loophole existed because nobody had noticed that the annex had been rewritten.

items.situation: Close the reconstruction: say what each side had done by the time the dispute reached the table.
tips: By the time the dispute reached the table, we had rejected the certificate and they had already invoiced the units.
Both sides had been working from a superseded revision, and neither had flagged it in time.

bank.label: Reconstructing the sequence (had + past participle)
items: By the time we caught the discrepancy, the supplier had already shipped the forty units.
The amendment had superseded revision C three months before the units left the factory.
Nobody had cross-checked the normative reference, so the discrepancy had gone unnoticed.

bank.label: Showing how long it had gone on (had been + -ing)
items: They had been manufacturing to a withdrawn revision for three months before the amendment reached them.
We had been inspecting against the wrong annex until the audit flagged it.
Both teams had been reading only the clause they already knew.

bank.label: Pinning down the fine print
items: The contract had stipulated the standard in its current revision, and that revision had changed.
Which document takes precedence here: the contract, or the standard it refers to?
The wording leaves a grey area, and I want that provision spelled out before we sign anything.

bank.label: Owning it without giving it away
items: We had not updated the specification in time, and I will say so; but you had not flagged the amendment either.
This is a shared discrepancy, not a breach: neither side had seen that the annex had moved.
Before we talk about cost, let us agree on the order of events, because the order decides the liability.
